text: BOTHENHAMPTON, a parish in Dorset, 1 m. SSE of Bridport. Area 600 acres. Pop. 533.

BOTHERAM BAY, an indentation of the W coast of the Red sea, in Abyssinia, in the district of Hodendowa, SSE of M. Gingerab, in N lat. 18° 45', E long. 37° 35'. It is studded with islets, and environed with shoals; but has good anchorage inside.
BOTHKENNAR, a small parish in Stirlingshire, in the carse of Falkirk, intersected by the Carron river. Pop. in 1801, 575; in 1841, 849.

BOTHNANG, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, in the bailiwick and about 2 m. bleacheries. BOTHNIA, [SWED. Botten or Botn; GERM. Bothnien,] a name given to the whole region lying around the gulf of Bothnia, on both sides, and as far S as to the straits called the Quarken. The district on the E side of the gulf, about 300 m. in length, and varying from 60 m. to 200 m. in breadth, was ceded to Russia in 1809; and now forms the circle of Uleäborg-Kuyana, in the grand-duchy of Finland. The remaining and larger portion belongs to Sweden; and is equal in area to a little more than one-third of the entire superficies of the kingdom. The Muonio river, throughout its whole course, to its junction with the Tornea, and the Tornea from this point of confluence to the sea, forms the boundary betwixt Russian and Swedish B. I. NORTHERN BOTHNIA, or NORR BOTTEN, is bounded on the N by the Norwegian bailiwick of Nordlands, and Russian Finland, or the district formerly known as Kemi Lapmark; on the E it is separated— as already stated—from Russian Finland by the Muonio and the Tornea rivers, and by the head of the gulf of B., to the mouth of the Pitea river, which, flowing in a SE and E direction, from the elevated mountain lake of Peskejaure, on the western declivity of the Norwegian Alps, near Mount Sulitelma, [alt. 6,178 ft.,] separates North B. over its whole S frontier from West B.; on the W it is separated from the Norwegian amt of Nordlands by that part of the great Norwegian chain sometimes distinguished as the Lapland Alps, in which Mount Sulitelma is the culminating point. Within the boundaries thus traced are comprehended Tornea Lapmark and Lulea Lapmark,—the former forming the northern half of North B.; and the latter, the southern. In the more recent administrative division of Sweden, the whole of North B. appears to be designated as the laen of Pitea, the superficial area of which is stated by Akrell to be 751 Swedish, or 1554.5 German, or 33,009 English sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 46,767. By far the greater portion of this region lies within the Arctic circle. II. WESTERN BOTHNIA, OF WESTER BOTTEN, is bounded on the N by North B.; on the E by the gulf of B. to about the parallel of 63\frac{1}{2}\text{o} N; on the S by the laen of Hernösands or Wester Norlands, and the laen of Oestersands or Jamtlands; and on the W by the Norwegian Alps, separating it from the amts of Nordre Trondhiem and Nordlands. Within these boundaries are comprised Pitea Lapmark, Umea Lapmark, and Asele Lapmark; the first forming the N; the second, the central; and the third, the S division of West B.—The superficial area of the whole is estimated by Akrell-whose tables are adopted in the report of the Statistical commission to government, of May 6th, 1844—at 668 Swedish, or 1382.7 German, or 29,361 English sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 57,164. In the recent administrative division of Sweden this region is designated as the laen of UMEA. In some maps, even of recent construction, all that portion of Bothnia which borders on the gulf of B., from Tornea S to the parallel of 63½° N, with an average breadth of from 30 to 40 geog. m., is designated Western B.; but we have followed the map of Stieler and Hübbe, founded on that of Carl von Forsell, and revised in 1846. In earlier geographical works-such as that of Busching-the division of North B. was not recognised; but the whole country on both sides of the gulf of B. was divided into East B. and West B.; whilst Lapland was divided into seven marks or provinces, five of which were placed under the administration of the governor of West B. This arrangement seems to be still retained in several BOTHNIA. W of Stuttgart. Pop. 1,130. It possesses some | maps. The following general remarks are applicable to B. or Lapland generally. General physical features.] The whole of B. may be described as a mountainous district. On the Norwegian, or W frontier, the mountains assume a terrific and sublime appearance,—the snow-line commencing at the elevation of 3,600 ft., and the greater part of the elevated regions presenting a naked expanse of desolate sterile rock. In the E districts, the mountains assume a less rugged appearance; yet, even here, the peculiarly wild features of the North are imprinted on the face of creation. Interminable heaths, unfathomable marshes, enormous lakes, headlong cataracts, dark forests, rugged and abrupt precipices, are everywhere seen. But there is here—as almost everywhere else-a peculiar and compensating beauty at certain seasons of the year, on which various travellers have eloquently expatiated. "The groves," says Dr. Clarke, "by the side of the Torneae, are delightful. A rude and devious path, always doubtful and often altogether indistinct, overshadowed by foliage impenetrable to the rays of the sun, now winding among rocks, now along the brink of a cataract, conducted us O'er many a wilder sweeter sod Than Fancy's feet have ever trod. In these woods, when removed from the noise of the cataracts, there is sometimes a stillness which is quite awful; it is the unbroken silence of Nature left entirely to herself; if it be interrupted, it is only so by the humming of the mosquitos, or the piping of the becassine, or the murmur of the wind. Man seems to be an intruder, for the first time, into the midst of solitudes that have never been trodden by any human foot: the very path which he pursues has not been traced by the footsteps of men, but of animals." Similar feelings were excited in Alfieri's mind by the scenery of Sweden. "Were I to choose a northern residence," says that poet, who visited Scandinavia in 1770, "I should prefer Sweden before any that I know. I was transported by the wild majesty of the boundless forests, its lakes and precipices, and, though I had not then read Ossian, many of his images were awakened within me, as I afterwards found on perusing him." In another place, the same Italian talks of a "certain inexpressible silence" which reigns in the atmosphere of Sweden, and which makes one think himself beyond the boundaries of earth. Climate.] The climate is of course severe, though milder than that of some other parts of the globe in the same latitude. For six weeks of the year, the country in the extreme north is veiled in the darkness of one uninterrupted night. But, as if to recompense the inhabitants of these regions, for such a privation, the sun, - 'As he nearly dips his flaming orb, Wheels up again and re-ascends the sky,' and, in some parts, remains above the horizon for six weeks. The heat of summer is also as excessive in degree, as the cold of winter. Rye and barley ripen in 66 days; and the growth of all vegetation is equally rapid. Lapland, according to Wahlenberg, may be divided into five zones or belts, concentric with the Bothnian gulf, and differing from each other in appearance, climate, and productions. The first, extending obliquely round the gulf, and generally 80 m. broad, is covered with forests of the spruce and Scotch fir. With the spruce-fir, the Rosa cinnamomea, the Convallaria bifolia, and the Galium boreale, also fail, and the Rubus arcticus, or dwarf-bramble, no longer ripens its fruit. The second, higher and colder than the first, and only from 6 to 8 m. broad, contains the Lapland, and is the most elevated tract of the whole, the greater part of it being above the limit of constant congelation: it produces no trees, and scarcely any vegetation whatever, except a few hardy plants where the snow has melted. In none of the three first zones is the height above the level of the sea considerable: the highest mountain in these three belts is only 1,140 ft. above the level of the sea. The church of Enontekis is 1,429 ft. above sea-level, and thence to the summit of the Lapland Alps the ground rises constantly, but so gradually that it is practicable to go in a boat to lake Kielisgarvi, which is in so elevated a situation that the birch-tree is scarcely to be found on its shores. The Lapland mountains are a continuation of the great chain which runs through Sweden and Norway, and extends in some of its branches to Finland and Russia. These mountains passing through the south of Lapland, and as high as the latitude of Tornea, are nearly of equal height through the whole of their extent, none being lower than 2,132 ft. above the level of the sea: but the most elevated mountains occur in the southern part of Luleä Lapmark. One of these-already noticed-has been long regarded, by the superstitious natives, with awe and veneration, being denominated in the Lapp language Sulitelma, i. e., 'the hill of God.' This Alp is situated in N lat. 67° 10', near the margin of a lake communicating with the Western ocean, and near a lake, on the opposite flank of the range which gives rise to the Pitea flowing into the gulf of B. It forms three peaks of the respective altitudes of 5,760, 5,870 and 6,178 English ft. These peaks are covered with an accumulation of eternal snow; and the sides of this mountain, at the alt. of 2,500 ft., exhibit real glaciers: the ice itself is perfectly clear and colourless, but its clefts appear blue. Near the base of this mountain, the lake Lommi-jauri, elevated 2,265 ft. above the level of the sea, has snow lying along its banks during the whole year. Half-a-degree farther N, Lake Viri-jauri, at an elevation of 1,900 ft., appears covered with hard ice in the middle of summer. These observations of Wahlenberg seem to harmonize with the theory which assigns from 2,230 to 2,325 ft. for the height of the mean boundary of congelation in these latitudes. To the N of Sulitelma is another immense glacier-bearing mountain, named Olmajalos, 5,543 ft. high; and Tulpajegna, a very extensive one, is 4,050 ft. in alt. Those of Gesetsjack, Pernetjack, and Ridaljack, to the N of Tulpajegna, are supposed to be little inferior in height; but from 68° 20' of N lat. the Alpine range, though continued in an almost uninterrupted line to the N cape, diminishes in elevation as it approaches the Frozen ocean. On the snowy Alps we find the Ranunculus nivalis, the Aira alpina, the Gentiana nivalis, and the Saxifraga nivalis. The gulf of B., and the high range of mountains which encircle the northern part of Sweden, preserve a certain equality of temp. over all B. in places of the same elevation. The following table exhibits the mean of a series of observations of temp. at Enontekis, Umea, and at Ulea, in degrees of Fahrenheit: Enontekis. Umea. Ulea.

BOTHNIA. It appears from the above table that at Enontekis, near the sources of the Muonio, in 68° 30½, and 1,429 ft. above sea-level, although the mean temp. is only 26°85, yet that of July is nearly sufficient to ripen corn; and in the register of variations at this place, given by Dr. Clarke, [Travels, vol. ix. p. 512] it is stated that the leaves of the birch began to appear on the 27th May; pasturage commenced in the forests on the 7th June; the first ear of barley appeared on the 26th of July; and birch-leaves began to turn yellow on the 23d of August. Excepting the fine spreading plants of the Rubus chamæmorus, all other vegetation diminishes in proportion to the distance N from Enontekis; but in going towards the S a very few miles cause a striking difference in the appearance of vegetation. It is only to the S of the parallel of 65°, however, and that but in certain favoured localities, that rye, barley, and oats, ripen with anything like regularity. Potatoes succeed tolerably well; but turnips and cabbages do not thrive. Rivers.] The rivers of B. commencing on the extreme N, and advancing S, are the Tornea, Luinio, Lulea, Pitea, Skelleftea, and Umea. Population.] B. is inhabited by Finlanders, Laplanders, and Swedes. The first of these are chiefly located along the banks of the Muonio and the Tornea. The Laplanders inhabit the interior districts. A few of them on the skirts of the lower ground around the gulf of B. have applied themselves to agriculture; but the greater part support themselves by their herds of reindeer, and fishing in the rivers and lakes. They are divided into districts; so many families composing a district. The Swedes occupy the coast-lands, and the broader valleys along the rivers.

BOTHNIA (GULF OF), the N arm of the Baltic sea, extending from the Aland islands to Tornea, or from the parallel of 60° N nearly to that of 66°. On the parallel of 60°, S of the Aland islands, its extreme width, from the Swedish coast of Stockholm laen to the coast of Russian Finland, S of the island of Kimito, is 120 geog. m.; but this breadth contracts in the space occupied by the Aland archipelago; and the largest island of that group leaves a channel called the Alandshaff of only 24 m. in breadth between its W coast and the coast of Sweden; and the Waltus Kiftet channel on the side of Finland is only 14 m. across. To the N of the Aland archipelago, the gulf gradually widens to about 120 geog. m.—the Penny Cyclop. says 240 m.—which breadth it retains to the parallel of 62°, where it begins to contract, gradually narrowing till it attains the parallel of 63° 30', when it has a breadth of only 50 m.; and throughout all that contracted part of it called the Quarken, lying between the parallels of 63° and 64°, its breadth varies from 50 to 60 m. N of the parallel of 64° it again expands; and on the parallel of 65°, has a breadth of about 100 m., which breadth decreases little to the head of the gulf. That portion of the gulf lying S of the Quarken is called the Bottniska haffet, i. e., 'Sea of Bothnia;' the portion to the N of the Quarken, the Bottniska wicken, i. e., 'Gulf of Bothnia.' -Estimating its entire length at 350 geog. m., and its mean breadth at 100 m., its superficies will be 35,000 sq. m. Berghaus estimates it at 1,840 German, or 39,072 English sq. m.—The coasts S of the Quarken are in general rocky, but not boldly so; to the N, they are low and sandy.—A strong current sets from the head of the gulf, through the Quarken, towards the larger isle of Aland, where it divides into two branches,—one taking the Swedish coast, the other the Russian. These currents again unite near Kækar point, and combine their stream with the constant current issuing from the gulf of Finland, which takes a direction towards the isle of Bornholm.—The line of the water-shed around this gulf is about an average distance of 150 m. from the shores. The many large rivers, and innumerable mountain-torrents, which descend into this gulf, at once freshening its waters, and by the current which they produce preventing the free ingress of the waters of See article Baltic. Von Buch, and latterly Lyell and others, are agreed in opinion that a gradual rise of the coasts of this gulf, unattended with any volcanic phenomena, is taking place, probably to the amount of 2 or 3 ft. in a cent. See article BALTIC .- The gulf of B. is generally covered with ice in winter, and this does not usually break up till the month of May. In 1809, a Russian brigade under Barclay de Tolly marched across the gulf from Wasa to Umea, accomplishing the distance in 3 days.

BOTHOA, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Guingamp. The cant. comprises 8 com. and contained in 1831, a pop. of 9,742. The village is 15 m. S of Guingamp. Pop. 2,538.
BOTHON. See BUTTEVANT.

BOTHWELL, a parish and village in Lanarkshire, on the N bank of the Clyde. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 3,017; in 1841, 11,175. This extraordinary increase in the pop. is attributed to the opening of iron and coal mines, the income arising from which is supposed to exceed £160,000 per ann.—The v. of B. is about 8 m. from Glasgow. Pop. in 1841, 490. This parish also contains the large v. of Bellshill and Uddingstone, and the town of Holytown. The fine ruins of B. castle are in the vicinity; which is also celebrated as the scene of the engagement between the Covenanters and the Duke of Monmouth on the 22d of June, 1679.
BOTICAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, 10 m. WSW of Chaves, and 50 m. E of Braganza.
BOTISMENE, a range of mountains in Madagascar, extending between the 21st and 24th parallels, and forming the S portion of the great chain by which the island is intersected from SSW to NNE.
BOTLEY, a parish in Southamptonshire, 4 m. SSE of Bishop's Waltham. Area 3,090 acres. Pop. 904.—Also a tything, partly in the p. of North Hinksey, partly in that of Cumnor, in Berks. Pop. 155.
BOTNA, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Bessarabia, which takes its rise in the mountainous district of Kichinev, runs to the SE, and unites with the Dniester, 6 m. SE of Bender.

BOTOA, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, which issues from the NW side of the Sierra Pedro, runs SSW, passes the town of the same name, opposite the junction of the Geborah, and unites with the Guadiana, a mile to the NE of Badajos. BOTOCUDOS [Southey], BOTECUDOS [Bullet. Geog.], or BOTUCUDOES, a singular and ferocious tribe of Brazil, the remains, it is supposed, of the once formidable Aymores. They appear to be chiefly located on the Rio Doce and the Rio Belmonte, from the parallel of 13° to 191° S lat. They are of middle stature; generally tall, robust, and well-proportioned; with high cheek bones, small eyes, a short nose with wide nostrils, and thick lips. Their colour is a reddish-brown, more or less clear, and sometimes varying to yellow. Their hair is strong, jet black, and shining. Their teeth are fine and white. They shave the back part of their head to about three inches above the ears, so as to leave only a little tuft on the top; but their most extraordinary practicethough not absolutely peculiar to this tribe-is that of inserting large pieces of wood in each ear and in the under lip. They call this lip-plug gnimato; and that of the ears huma; but the Portuguese call it vatoque, or botoque; whence the name by which they are designated amongst Europeans. They call themselves Crecmun,

CRACMUN, or ENGEREEKMUNG. The botoque is generally inserted at the age of seven or eight. For this purpose the lobes of the ears, and the lower lip, are stretched; holes are then made in them with a sharp-pointed stick, and small pieces of wood are at first inserted, for which larger pieces are successively substituted, until the desired elongation is perfected. The wood used is that of the Bombyx ventricosa, or wool-tree, which is lighter than cork, and very white. These plugs-which measure 11 inch. in diam. -weigh down the lip in old persons; in earlier life, they give it a horizontal direction, in which the lip appears only like a thin ring encircling the upper part of the botoque. They are removable at pleasure; the lip then falls and shows the lower teeth through the hole. The other adornments of the B. are necklaces made of hard berries, or the teeth of animals, and diadems or bunches of feathers; round the neck they wear a knife attached to a strong cord; and by way of full dress, or military costume perhaps, they occasionally paint their bodies black and their faces red. They are reputed to be anthropophagi; and they appear to hold almost the very lowest rank even amongst South American savages, in point of morals or of religious ideas. Their language differs considerably from that of the neighbouring tribes. It abounds in vowels and nasal sounds, but has no gutturals except the hard c BOT or k. M. Marcus Porte has given a brief vocabulary of it in the Bulletin de la Soc. Geog. for 1846. M. F. Denis, in his Hist. et descript. du Bresil, in the Univers pictoresque, states that many of the B. have been reclaimed from utter barbarism, and induced to renounce the use of the botoque, by the efforts of a French officer in the Brazilian army called Guido Marlière.

BOTOE-NOER, a lake of Denmark, in the S of the island of Falster. It is about 10 m. in length, and 13 m. in average breadth.—A town of the same name is situated on the narrow neck of land to the E of the above lake, and 7 m. SE of Nyekioping.

BOTO-FOGO, a pleasant village, or rather cluster of villas, 3 m. S of Rio-Janeiro, at the base of the lofty mountains of the Corcuvado [alt. 1,580 ft.], and commanding a fine view of the mouth of the harbour. It is altogether the most attractive spot in the environs of the cap. of Brazil.
BOTOKE, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, in the district and 10 m. W of Rossieny, and 33 m. NE of Tilset.

BOTOL, or Botel Tabago XIMA (GREAT), an island of the Chinese sea, SE of the island of Formosa, and N of the Bashi group, in N lat. 22°, and E long. 121° 40'. It is very elevated, and appears to be well wooded, from the third part of its height to the summit. [La Perouse.] It contains several villages.—To the S by E is a smaller and apparently desert island named LITTLE BOTOL TOBAGO. The passage between the two islands is from 4 to 5 m. in width.
BOTONGA, a district of SE Africa, extending NW from the coast along the r. bank of the Zambesi. It is almost entirely unknown to Europeans.

BOTSKI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Grodno, dist. and 12 S of Bielsk, on the r. bank of the Nourshik. Pop. 1,462, of whom a large proportion are Jews. It contains a castle and several convents.
BOTRIPHNIE, a parish in Banffshire, stretching along the Isla. Pop. in 1801, 589; in 1841, 740.
BOTSORREL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Ponthou. Pop. 1,359.
BOTTE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Lower Seine, com. of Eprétot. Pop. 26.
BOTTENDORF, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, regency of Merseburg, 10 m. SW of Querfurt. Pop. 889. It has a copper foundry, and in the environs is a copper mine.
BOTTESFORD, a parish in Leicestershire, in the vale of Belvoir, 7 m. WNW of Grantham. Area 5.010 acres. Pop. 1,375.—Also a parish in Lincoln, 7 m. W of Glandford Bridge. Area 8,210 acres. Pop. 1,586.
BOTTESLAW, a township in the p. of Stokeupon-Trent, 2 m. E of Newcastle. Pop. 80.
BOTTIDA, a village of Sardinia, at the foot of Monte Corona, a spur of the Monteraso range. Pop. 600.
BOTTINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 3 m. E of Münsingen, in the Hardt. Pop. 570. In the environs are some marble quarries.
BOTTISHAM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 6 m. WSW of Newmarket. Area 4,700 acres. Pop. 1,497.
BOTTLE HILL, a village of Chatham township, in Morris co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 57 m. NE of Trenton.

BOTTOMLESS PIT, a tract of sea off Picaniny Bassam, on the Ivory coast of W Africa, in N lat. 5° 15', W long. 3° 57'. On each side of it are from 40 to 70 fath.; but no soundings have been obtained within the gully thus enclosed between two banks.
BOTTOMLESS ISLE, or Sondre-Grond, an island off the coast of Peru, discovered by Schouten in 1616, in S lat. 15° 15', 100 leagues W of the Island of Dogs.
BOTTWAR (GRoss), a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, in the bailiwick and 5 m. NNE of Marbach, on the l. bank of the Bottwar. Pop. 2,495. The environs afford excellent wine.
BOTTWNOG, a parish in Carnarvonshire, 7 m. SW by W of Pwllheli. Pop. 191.
BOTUS FLEMING, a parish in Cornwall, 3 m. NW by N of Saltash. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 250.

BOTUSHANI, a town of Turkey in Europe, the cap. of a district of the same name, in the prov. of Moldavia, 13 m. NNW of Girlau, and 55 m. NNW of Jassy. Pop. 4,500, consisting of Greeks, Armenians, and Jews. It contains several churches and synagogues. The houses are generally built of wood, and have gardens and trees interspersed. Its commerce, which is extremely active, consists in wine, cattle, wool, honey, wax, and tobacco, all of which are extensively exported to Germany. The fairs held in this town are the most important in Moldavia.—The district of B. is watered and bounded on the E by the Jijia, and on the W by the Sereth.

BOTZEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, in the district and 6 m. WNW of Brugg. Pop. 485. BOTZEN, BOZZEN, BOLZANO, or BULSANO, a town of Austria, in the Tyrol, the cap. of a circle of the same name, 58 m. S of Innspruck. It is situated amid mountains, at the confluence of the Talfer with the Eisach, at an alt. of 1,100 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 7,084, of whom the majority are Italians. B. is built in the Italian style; and contains a castle, several convents, a college, and a gymnasium. It has manufactories of silk, linen, and various woollen fabrics; and has a considerable transit-commerce. The fairs, which are held here four times a-year, are much frequented by Germans, Swiss, and Italians. B. occupies the site of the ancient Pons Drusi.—The circle of B., called also the circle of the Adige, comprises the valleys of the Adige and Eisach. It contains 3 cities, 3 towns, and 310 villages; and a pop. of 99,782, chiefly Italians. It contains no lofty mountains. The soil, although marshy, is fertile, and produces wine, fruit, grain, and hemp. Cattle are reared in great numbers; and the culture of the silk-worm is becoming an important object of local industry. Wooden-ware and lace are the chief articles of manufacture.
BOTZENBURG. See BOITZENBURG.
BOTZINGEN. See BOUJEAN.

BOTZLINGEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Uri, in a large and fertile meadow near the r. bank of the Reuss, 3 m. S of Altorf. The annual meeting of the landsgemeinde, or cantonal assembly, is held in the immediate vicinity of this village, in the Prairie du Gand.
BOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. and 12 m. from Orleans. Pop. 674. The environs afford good red wines.
BOUAFLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Meulan, 15 m. from Versailles. Pop. 1,092.
BOUALI. See Loango.
BOUAN-SIMA, an island of the China sea, at the entrance of the gulf of Tonquin, and near the E coast of the empire of Anam, in N lat. 18° 45', E long. 106° 15'.

BOUAYE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Loire, arrond. of Nantes. The cant. comprises 7 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,896. The vil. is 9 m. SW of Nantes. Pop. 1,297.

BOUAN, or BUAN, a small inhabited island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Sulu sea, near the NE coast of the island of Borneo, in N lat. 6° 15', E long. 118° 15'. It contains a saline lake, and is well wooded. I. BOU
BOUBNOV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Poltova, in the district and 18 m. W of Zolotonocha, near the l. bank of the Dnieper.
BOUCE', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Ecouché, 9 m. SW of Argentan. Pop. 1,533. It possesses some iron-works.

BOUCHAIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Valenciennes. -The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,851; in 1841, 28,205.—The town is situated on the Schelde, 13 m. SE of Douay. Pop. 1,183. Besides salt-refineries and tanneries, it has manufactories of sugar from beet-root. Coal forms the chief export of local produce. Cattle fairs are held monthly.—This town was built in the 8th cent. by Pepin; and was formerly the cap. of the county of Ostrevand. It is a place of great strength; the surrounding country can be laid under water by means of sluices. The French took it in 1676, and preserved it at the peace of Nimeguen. It was taken in 1711, after a memorable siege, by the Allies; but was given back to France at the peace of Utrecht. In 1793 and 1794 the country in the neighbourhood of B. was the scene of a number of severe conflicts between the Austrians and French. The district of which B. was formerly the cap. was called Astavan. The fortress was one of those occupied by the Allies after the peace of 1815.

BOUCHARD'S ISLES, a group of islands in the St. Lawrence, opposite the seignories of Vercheres and Contrecoeur, the largest of which is about 5 m. in length, and 1 m. in average breadth. They contain good soil, and present some good meadow and pasture land; but are in their greater extent covered with wood. These islands form the port of St. Sulpice.
BOUCHEMAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Aubrey, on the r. bank of the Loire, at the junction of the Maine. Pop. 1,333.

BOUCHERVILLE, a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Chambly, on the southern side of the St. Lawrence. The soil, which is light, and generally well-cultivated, produces good average crops of all sorts. Two small streams that fall into the St. Lawrence partially water the westerly portion of the S. The village of the same name is pleasantly situated on the St. Lawrence, and on the main road to Chambly. It contains about 100 houses, irregularly, but in some instances well-built, a church and parsonage house, a chapel, convent, and boys' school. Amongst the residents are many of the descendants of the ancient French nobility. The parish of B. includes, in addition to the S of that name, a portion of the S of Montarville.
BOUCHES DE CATTARO. See Bocca-di-Cat- TARO.

BOUCHES-DU-RHONE, a department in the SE of France, situated between the parallels of 43° 8' and 43° 55' N, and stretching along the coast of the Mediterranean, by which it is bounded on the SSW; on the NW bounded by the Rhone, which separates it from the dep. of Gard; on the NE, by the Durance, which separates it from the dep. of Vaucluse; and on the E, by the dep. of Var. Its superficial area is 512,991 hectares, [511,830 h. according to Guibert, or 1,267,674 acres, being nearly equal to that of the county of Norfolk.-The N and E districts are hilly, and in some parts mountainous, being intersected by spurs of the Estérel and the Maritime Alps. The highest summit of the latter is that of Mont Sainte-Victoire, between the Durance and the Arc, 1,042 metres, or 3,418 ft. in alt. [Guibert], 965 met. [Dict. Geog.] On the W and S it has extensive alluvial plains, and the coast is bordered with marshes, and numerous etangs or lagunes. The extensive 3 I plains called the Crau and the Camargue merit particular notice even in this brief description.-The Crau, or Craou, is a vast stone-covered plain, lying between the mountains and the sea, the Rhone, and the etangs of Martigues. It is covered with boulders or rolled stones of all sizes, from that of a pea to that of a man's head, which the natives call sistres, forming in fact a pure shingle, without intermixture of earth or sand. Of course the greater proportion of the Crau is uninhabited, and entirely sterile. Vegetation is rare and miserable: absinthium and lavender so low and poor as scarcely to be recognised; two or three miserable grasses, with Centaurea calycitropes, and C. solstitialis, form the principal traces of vegetable life over the greater portion of its surface in summer; but under the stones a short sweet herbage is found in some spots, which supplies winter-pasturage to sheep; and by means of the Canal de Craponne, which has been led from the Durance on the E, to Arles on the Rhone, and from which branches are carried to the Etang de Dezcaunies, and the Etang de l'Olivier, the irrigation of some parts of this sterile region has been accomplished, and corn and meadowland formed, presenting "an amazing contrast between the soil in its natural and in its watered state." [Young.]—The Camargue is a large island in the delta of the Rhone; having the city of Arles at its apex; the Petit Rhone and the Canal de Silvereal on its W side; and the larger arm of the Rhone on the E. Its soil is a fine humus, resting on a substratum of calcareous debris, beneath which is a bed of seasand. A large portion of its surface is occupied with marshes and etangs, of which the most considerable is the Etang de Valcarès, which, with the Marais de Valcares, on its W side, covers nearly one-half of the whole surface.—The Etang de Berre, communicating with the sea by the Port de Bouc, or Canal des Martigues, and receiving on its E side the river Arc, and the Touloubre on the N, is a large sheet of water. See Berre.—Between the last-mentioned etang on the E, and the E arm of the Rhone on the W, is the Etang des Landes, communicating with the sea by the Etang de Galejon; and SSE of Arles, on the E side of the Crau, is the Etang de Baux, communicating with the Rhone.—The coast-line of this dep. is sinuous, and presents seven small harbours, besides that of Marseilles. A small cluster of 11 islets lies off Marseilles. The climate is dry and warm. The mistral, blowing from the NW, that is from the elevated region of the Cevennes, is often severely felt. January is the coldest month.—The vegetable productions of this dep. include the olive, the fig, the mulberry, the melon, the almond, and other fruits of the warm districts of Europe; while the cypress, the laurel, the myrtle, the cistus, and the phillyrea, flourish luxuriantly. The mountains are covered with thyme, lavender, hyssop, sage, rosemary, and other aromatic herbs. About 106,000 hectares are under cultivation; 160,199 are occupied with etangs and marshes; 183,000 with hills and mountains; and 64,000 with woods and forests. A thirteenth part of the surface is devoted to the cultivation of the vine; and the territory of Roquevaire is celebrated for its Vins muscats and its dried raisins. The produce of silk in 1835 was 330,000 kilog.; of wine 500,000 hectol. Immense flocks of sheep and goats are reared. The number of sheep in 1830 was 551,377; the number of horses is about 10,000; that of horned cattle from 3,000 to 4,000.—The mineral productions are iron, antimony, marble, treestone, and coal.—The manufacturing industry of the dep. is considerable. Soap is one of the most important articles of manufacture; soda is likewise made in large quantities; leather, cotton and woollen goods, liqueurs, essences, oil, vinegar, brandy, and wine are also BOU largely produced.—The interior commerce is considerable; but is greatly exceeded by the exterior, Marseilles, the cap. of the dep., being one of the chief exporting ports in the kingdom. See article MARSEILLES.—Traffic is aided by the canal of Arles from that city to the Port de Bouc, and the Canal de Craponne already noticed, which, besides the branches intersecting the Crau towards Istier, sends off a branch to the N called the Canal de Real, and another to the Touloubre. The pop. of this dep. in 1801, was 285,012; in 1821, 313,614; in 1836, 362,325; in 1846, 413,918. The last number gives 80.69 inhabitants to the sq. kilom.; the mean for all France being 67.088. This dep. ranks therefore as the 17th in point of density of pop.; that of Moselle being the 16th, which has 84.10 to the sq. kilom.; and Haute-Garonne, which has 77.91, the 18th. Of this pop. the number of the age of 25 who could read and write was 47.48 in 100. The number at school in 1840 was only 1 in 15.33 of the pop. This dep. is divided into 3 arrondissements or subprefectures, viz., Marseilles, Aix, and Arles, which are subdivided into 27 cantons, and 106 communes. The number of places having a pop. exceeding 3,000, in 1836, was 16. The dep. forms the two dioceses of the bishop of Marseilles, and the archb. of Aix. Its superior tribunal is that of the cour royale of Aix .- The amount of impots directs levied from this dep. in 1839 was 2,731,405 francs.

BOUCHET (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine and Oise, com. of Vert-le-Petit, on the Juine, near its confluence with the Essonne. Pop. 83. It contains an extensive powder-mill. The castle of B. was erected in favour of the celebrated Duquesne into a marquisate.
BOUCHOUX (LES), or Bonneville-Les-Bou-
CHOUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of St. Claude. The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831, a pop. of 6,025. The vil. is 8 m. SSW of Saint-Claude.
BOUCK, a river of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, which unites with the Meuse 9 m. S of Namur.
BOUDAKOV BOUERAK, a settlement of German colonists in Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Saratov and dist. of Kamichin, on the r. bank of the Volga.
BOUDARINSKOI, a town and fort of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Orenburg, on the r. bank of the Ural, 45 m. SSW of Uralsk.
BOUDEUSE (LA), an island of the Indian ocean, in the Amirantes group, in S lat. 6° 12', and E long. 52° 48′.
BOUDILSKOI POROG, a cascade of the Dnieper, in the gov. and 30 m. S of Jekaterinoslav.
BOUDITCHE, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 22 m. N of Poltova, in the dist. of Zenkov, near the r. bank of the Vorskla.
BOUDJIAK. See BUDJIAK.
BOUDREVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote d'Or, cant. of Montigny, on the r. bank of the Aube, 15 m. ENE of Chatillon-sur-Seine. Pop. 300. It possesses some iron works.
BOUDROUN. See Bodroun.

BOUDRY, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. SW of Neufchatel and near the entrance of the Reuse into the lake of that name. Pop. 1,500. It has a calico manufactory and two annual fairs. The environs afford good red wines, and contain quarries of gypsum.
BOUDSLAV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Minsk, in the dist. and 21 m. NE of Vileika, on the r. bank of the Servetsch.

BOUDUI-RUIBINSKAIA, a town of Russia in CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CO Europe, in the gov. and 37 m. from Koursk, in the dist. and 16 m. W of Oboian.
BOUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Nouviou. Pop. 1,434.
BOUERE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Griz-en-Bouire. Pop. 1,847.
BOUEXIERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Liffre. Pop. 2,016. It has some blast furnaces.

BOUFFARIK, a district and village of N Africa, in Algeria, in the prov. and 20 m. SSW of Algiers. Pop. in 1838, 500; in 1846, 2,131. It has wellfrequented cattle-markets, and the Camp d'Erlon here forms an important military station on the road to Blidah and Oran.
BOUFFIOULX, a commune and village of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 4 m. E of Charleroi, near the Sambre. Pop. 1,027. Pottery-ware is made here.
BOUFFLERS. See CRILLON.
BOUFFLERS (CAPE), the S point of a bight on the S coast of Australia, bearing S 36° E from West Cape Howe.
BOUG. See Bog, and Bug.
BOUGADITZA. See BUGADITZA.

BOUGAINVILLE BAY, or TEXADA BAY [Cordova], a small bay in the strait of Magalhaens, on the coast of Patagonia, opposite Nassau island, forming "a basin or wet dock in which a vessel might careen with perfect security." [King.] It is about 426 yards long, and of great depth; and is surmounted by high mountains which defend it against all winds. Bougainville, the French navigator, repaired his vessels here, and spent some time on shore, in December 1767.
BOUGAINVILLE (CAPE), a cape on the E coast of Van Diemen's Land, 5 m. from Port Montbazin, in S lat. 42° 30'.

BOUGAINVILLE'S ISLAND, a large island in the archipelago of New Georgia, or the Solomon group, in the Pacific, in S lat. 6°. It is about 60 m. in length; and from 5 to 10 m. in breadth. It is high and mountainous, and its coasts are of difficult access. It was discovered on the 30th of June, 1768, by the navigator whose name it bears.
BOUGHROOD, a parish in Radnorshire, 7 m. SW by W of Hay, in a picturesque valley on the N bank of the Wye. Pop. 322.

BOUGHTON, a parish in Norfolk, 11 m. N of Stoke Ferry. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 209.—Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. N of Northampton. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 389.—Also a p. in Nottinghamshire, 2 m. NE of Ollerton. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 309.
BOUGHTON-ALUPH, a parish in Kent, 4 m. NE of Ashford. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. 524.
BOUGHTON - UNDER - BLEAN, a parish in Kent, 3 m. SSE of Faversham. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 1,373.
BOUGHTON (GREAT), a township in the p. of St. Oswald, in Cheshire. Area 840 acres. Pop. 949.
BOUGHTON-MALHERB, a parish in Kent, 11 m. ESE of Maidstone. Area 2,590 acres. Pop. 512.
BOUGHTON-MONCHELSEA, a parish in Kent, 4 m. N by E of Maidstone. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 1,106.
BOUGIE. See Bugia.
BOUGLON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, arrond. and 8 m. SSW of Marmande. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. of com. 344.
BOUGOULMA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, 150 m. WSW of Ufa, on the Bougoulminka. Pop. 1,600. Important fairs are held here. \ BOU
BOUGOUROUSLAN, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, on the r. bank of the Kenil. Pop. 1,500.
BOUGULNAIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Boriage, on the l. bank of the Loire, 4 m. SW of Nantes. Pop. 3,287.
BOUI, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 60 m. N of Kostroma, on the Kostroma, at the embouchure of the Voksa. Pop. 800.
BOUILH-D'ARRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Pouvastruc. Pop. 329.
BOUILLAC, or Boullac, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Carbon - Blanc. Pop. 663.—Also a commune in the dep. of Tarn-et- Garonne, cant. of Verdun-sur-Garonne. Pop. 1,298.
BOUILLANCOURT-EN-SERIC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Gamaches. Pop. 1,140.
BOUILLANTE, a town in the island of Guadeloupe, on the W coast, 8 m. NNW of Basse Terre, at the mouth of a small stream of the same name. There are several hot sulphur springs in the vicinity.
BOUILLARGUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Nismes. Pop. 2,695.

BOUILLE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Grand-Couronne, on the l. bank of the Seine, 9 m. SW of Rouen. It has some commerce in building materials and wood by means of the river. Pop. 1,171.
BOUILLE-LORET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Argenton-Chateau, 8 m. NNW of Thouars. Pop. 1,088.

BOUILLON, a duchy in Belgium, lying between the grand duchy of Luxemburg and the principality of Liege. It is about 18 m. long, and 9 m. broad; and lies in the woody and mountainous tract called the Ardennes, to the dep. of which name it for the most part belonged while in the French empire. It comprises the towns and villages of Bouillon, Miruart, St. Hubert, Rochefort, Logne, and Hiergues.—This small state has been at different times the subject of litigation. The celebrated Godfrey, general of the first crusade, and king of Jerusalem, mortgaged it, when a principality, to the bishopric of Liege, in 1096, for the sum of 1,500 silver marks; on condition that if he returned, the estate was to be restored, otherwise to remain attached to the diocese. Godfrey dying in the Holy Land without issue, his estate remained in the possession of the see of Liege, until 1483, when it was ceded to the ferocious count of Mark; the emperor Charles V. however restored it to its former owners. The French family of La Tour d'Auvergne, descendants of Charlotte de la Mark, the sister and heiress of William Robert de la Mark, brought forward at different periods pretensions to this duchy, which they relinquished in 1641, on receiving an indemnity of £15,000. But the bishop of Liege having taken part against France in the war of 1672, Louis put the family of La Tour d'Auvergne in possession of the duchy of B. The title of prince of B. was assumed in 1792, with the consent of his Britannic Majesty, by Philip d'Auvergne, captain in the royal navy, and he continued to bear it till his death which happened in 1816. The congress, which met at Vienna in 1815, assigned the sovereignty of the duchy to the king of the Netherlands, as grand-duke of Luxemburg; and appointed commissioners to investigate the comparative claims of Captain d'Auvergne and Prince Charles of Rohan. This commission decided in favour of the latter, who, in 1822, ceded his rights to the king of the Netherlands for an annual payment of 5,000 crowns. The treaty of 19th April, 1839, assigned | this duchy to Belgium.

BOUILLON, the chief town of the foregoing duchy, lies in a gorge of the Ardennes, on the l. bank of the Semoy, near its junction with the Maese; 18 m. WSW of Neufchateau. It is a small but neatly built place; and has a castle, which, though standing on a high rock, and provided with strong works, is of little service, as it is commanded by the neighbouring hills. The town contained, in 1835, 2,509 inhabitants; in 1847, 2,870. In May 1794, the Austrian general Beaulieu, having forced a body of French troops to take refuge in the castle of B., pursued his advantage, and made an attack on the town, which surrendered after an obstinate conflict, and was given up to pillage. But the advance of a French army into the duchy of Luxemburg forced the Austrians to retreat in the direction of Namur. The castle was re-edified in 1827-30.
BOUILLY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, arrond. of Troyes. The cant. comprises 29 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,818. Pop. of com. 826.
BOUIN, a small island and commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, at the bottom of the bay of Bourgneuf. Area 300 hectares, or 741 acres. Pop. 2,640.

BOUINSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 40 m. NNW of Simbirsk, on the Karla. Pop. 3,168, chiefly Tartars. BOUJAH, BUDJAH, or BOUJA, a delightful village 3 m. from Smyrna, chiefly inhabited by families of European merchants born in Smyrna, and by treaty under the protection of their respective governments. Mount Corax rises in the neighbourhood, backed by the splendid heights of Tmolus, now called Bousdag. On the N is Mount Sipylus; and on the S the range of Dactyle. There is an English chapel here.
BOUJEAILLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Levier. Pop. 1,060.
BOUJEAN, or Botzingen, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, on the Süss, 13 m. NE of Bienne. Pop. 700.
BOUJOUK-DERE. See BUJUKDERE.
BOUKA. See Anson's Island.
BOUKENA, or Bokani, a town of Moldavia, on the Baglia river, 30 m. NW of Jassy.
BOUKEO, a river of Tibet, which unites at Sungnam, with the Durhony, and forms the Ruskolang, a tributary of the Sutledge.
BOUKHARA. See BOKHARA.
BOUKHOREST. See BUCHAREST.
BOULAK. See BULAK.

BOULAY, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Moselle, arrond. of Metz. - The cant., comprising 33 com., had a pop. of 17,286 in 1831.—The com., 18 m. NE of Metz, on the Kultzbach, has a pop. of 2,689, of whom a large proportion are Jews.

BOULARDRIE ISLAND, an island of Cape Breton, at the entrance of the Bras d'Or. It is about 20 m. in length, and from 1 to 2 m. in breadth; and stretches longitudinally between the two channels known as the Great and the Little Bras d'Or. It is somewhat populously inhabited by Scotch Highlanders and Irish fishermen.
BOULBON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bouches-de-Rhone, cant. of Tarascon. Pop. 1,041.
BOULDER ISLAND, a small island in Camden bay, on the Arctic coast, in N lat. 70° 5', W long. 144° 50'.
BOULDON, a township in the p. of Holdgate, in Salop, 7 m. NNE of Ludlow. Area 240 acres. Pop. 61.
BOULEKOMBA, or BOELEKOMBA, a town at the BOU SE extremity of the island of Celebes, at the embouchure of the Kalikongang.
BOULGARIA. See BULGARIA.
BOULGE, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. NNW of Woodbridge. Area 700 acres. Pop. 45.
BOULLANGER (CAPE), or COXCOMB HEAD, the N extremity of Maria island, Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 42° 34', and E long. 148° 14'.
BOULLERET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Lere. Pop. 1,437.
BOULMER, a township in the p. of Long Houghton, in Northumberland, 6 m. E of Alnwick. Pop. with Seaton House, 153.

BOULOGNE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Vendee, runs NNW, and falls, after a course of 50 m., of which 6 m. are navigable, into the S extremity of the Lake de Grand-Lieu .- Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Neuilly, on the r. bank of the Seine, 5 m. W of Paris. Pop. 5,391. It is pleasantly situated on the confines of the wood from which it takes its name, and is surrounded by many fine country-houses. It has manufactories of eau de javelle, and sealing-wax, and numerous bleacheries. The promenades of the Bois de Boulogne are the finest and among the most frequented in the environs of Paris.

BOULOGNE, or Bouloigne, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Upper-Garonne, arrond. of St. Gaudens. The cant. comprises 25 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 11,961. The town stands on the summit of a hill 15 m. NNW of St. Gaudens. Its commerce consists in grain, chestnuts, yarn, iron-wire, and culinary preparations of geese and ducks which are reared in great numbers in the locality.

BOULOGNE, or Boulogne-sur-Mer, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais.—The arrond. comprises 6 cant., viz., B., Calais, Desvres, Guines, Marquise, and Samer; and has a superficial area of 93,981 hectares. Its pop. in 1836 was 105,465; in 1846, 117,900.—The cant. comprises 8 com.; and had a pop. in 1831 of 26,274; in 1846, of 29,741. Boulogne-sur-Mer, the cap. of the above arrond. and cant., is situated on the coast, at the mouth of the Liane, 21 m. SSW of Calais, 66 m. NW of Arras, and 1351 m. NNW of Paris; by railway 75 m. from Amiens, and 166 m. from Paris; in N lat. 50° 43′ 33″, E long. 1° 36′.—B. consists of two towns, -an upper and a lower; the former is the older part, and occupies the summit of a hill; it is encircled by ramparts, with arched gateways, and has an old castle at its E angle, divided from the town by a fosse, but modernised and now converted into barracks. The lower or new town stretches along the r. bank of the Liane, from the old town to the harbour, and along the shore. It is regularly built, and contains the best streets and houses. On the l. bank of the river is a suburb called Copecure. The Haute ville, or upper town, contains the hotel-de-ville, the palais-de-justice, an antique belfry, and the maison d'arret or prison. Agreeable promenades have been formed on the ramparts. The Basse ville, or lower town, contains an interesting museum, and the public library of 24,000 vols., and 300 manuscripts. There are two English Episcopal chapels, and a Wesleyan chapel in B.; also an hospital, and several charitable institutions. The etablissement des bains, on the margin of the fine sandy beach, contains a magnificent and well-furnished set of baths, reading-rooms, and assembly-rooms.-On a hill to the W of B., at about \( \frac{1}{4} \) m. distance, is the column of Napoleon. Its total height is 164 ft.; diam. 13 ft.; a bronze statue of the emperor in his coronation robes, by Bosio, 16 flight of steps leading up to it on each side. It is The harbour of B. is formed in the channel of the Liane, with the addition of a semicircular basin on the l. bank of the river, which was originally dug to contain Napoleon's celebrated flotilla intended for the invasion of England. It was formerly dry at low water, and is still somewhat difficult of access; but has of late years been much improved by the construction of new piers and other works. The entrance is bounded by two jetties. That to the W is solid up to the level of high water, and is thrown out along the line of the rocky shore, a length of 1,970 ft. The other to the E runs directly out into the sea; and the bottom of the intervening channel is 230 ft. in width. Spring-tides rise 24 ft.; and neaptides 17 ft.; but when it blows from the W, the springs rise 28 ft.; and the neaps 19 ft. It will be seen from the subjoined tables that this port is the great place of debarkation for passengers between England and the continent, the route by B. being the most expeditious to Paris, and B. itself having become in fact "one of the chief British colonies abroad." [Murray's Handbook.] A keen rivalry, however, exists between it and Calais with respect to the packet-service betwixt night with westerly winds; that the communication by boats is more difficult than at Calais, and that several fatal accidents have occurred in B. bay even by daylight; and, finally, that apart from the difficulties of the harbour, the passage itself is much more dangerous, the course lying athwart the track of ships running up channel, and also among the fishing-vessels. Stress is also laid upon the waste of fuel and wear of vessel and machinery, from the exposed nature of the coast rendering it frequently unsafe to anchor. For all these causes B. is represented as a port fit for fine weather only. As regards Calais, it is described in terms directly opposite. It is sheltered from W and SW winds; the harbour is easy of entrance; and there is no difficulty in landing in boats or lying at anchor. The coast, too, is perfectly safe to approach in thick weather, shoaling gradually towards the shore, so that a vessel may feel her way; the piers are favourably constructed; the light is brilliant; the channel wide from pier to pier; and the sea one that diminishes in violence as you near the coast, owing to the shelter of Cape Grisnez. These representations may not perhaps be of so great weight as to compensate for the loss of time in travelling over 26 m. of common road between Paris and Calais. We believe it has been decided by the post-office authorities of both countries that the quick mail between London and Paris is to be carried via B.; but that from the 15th of November to the 15th of March, Calais is to be the packet-port.—The following tables, illustrative generally of the intercourse between England and the continent, will find more appropriate insertion here than under the head of any of the other continental ports. The number of passengers between the English ports and the principal continental ports on the opposite coast, was as follows in 1847 and 1848: Dieppe, . Dieppe, . Ostend, . Total, . Total, . Of 100 passengers, 74.8 per cent. went through the French and 25.2 through the Belgian ports, in 1847. In 1848 the proportions were 78.1 and 21.9 per cent. Of the 74.8 through the French ports in 1847, B. and Calais took 59.1; and of the 78.1 in 1848, 70.3. Finally, of 100 passengers by B. and Calais, 82.5 went through B. in 1847, and 80.5 in 1848. The number of passengers, carriages, and horses, landed and embarked at B. and Calais, in 1848, was as under: Carriages. Horses. Passengers. Out. In. Out In. In. Out. Boulogne, In the same year, 1,158 steamers entered, and 1,149 left, Boulogne; 638 entered, and 637 left, Calais. The following table of the number of passengers in and out from B., presents a curious view of the effect of the various political events of the year 1848 on the international traffic. Passeng. in. Passeng. out. Total. The proportions of arrivals and departures at each port during the several months is about the same as at B., the departures greatly exceeding the arrivals in the two or three months following the February revolution. Of the 69,909 passengers through B. in 1848, 46,301 were to or from Folkestone; 14,004 London; 9,353 Dover; and 251 to or from other ports; 47,962 were British subjects, 13,757 French, 2,670 Germans of different denominations, 1,654 Italians of different denominations, 1,391 subjects of the United States of America, and 2,475 subjects of other countries. [Daily News.] The pop. of B. in 1789 was 8,814; in 1821, 16,607; in 1831, 20,856; in 1846, 29,741. Of this pop. nearly one-sixth are English.—The manufactures of B. consist of ropes, fishing-nets, leather, bricks, linen and woollen stuffs, and bottles. The town carries on an active trade in fresh and salt fish, of which vast quantities are transported to Paris. Herrings, mackarel, whiting, turbot, cod, thornback, soles, eels, and oysters, are the principal kinds caught by the B. fishermen; lobsters, crabs, cray-fish, and John-Dories, are also taken.—The quality of the land in the environs of B. varies considerably, being composed of gravelly. clayey, ferruginous, and marshy soils. The clayey soil is well adapted for wheat; the sandy soil, which is in the least proportion, produces rye, barley, and potatoes. The larger farms in the neighbourhood rarely exceed 100 hectares = 247 acres; and in general run from 35 to 60 hect. The average rent of pasture-land in 1834 was from 48s. to 80s.; of grainland 24s. to 40s. History.] The name by which B. was known to the Romans was Gessoriaeum, which first occurs in Pomponius Mela. About the commencement of the Christian era, a fort was built a little to the N of Gessoriacum, on the other side of the Elna or Liane, to which the name of Bononia was given; and this name was ultimately identified with the town itself. The Normans sacked B. in 882. Henry of England made a fruitless attack upon it in 1347. Henry made himself master of it in 1544; and Edward VI. restored it to France for 400,000 crowns. After the destruction of Thérouane by Charles V., in 1553, the episcopal seat was transported to B., where it existed till 1789. Napoleon elevated B. into celebrity by making it the rendezvous of the 'Grande Armée' destined for the invasion of England in 1804. The port of B. was unsuccessfully attacked by Nelson on the 15th of August, 1801; and by Admiral Keith on the 10th of August, 1804; but on the defeat of the combined French and Spanish fleets in 1805, the camp at B. was broken up, and the troops were marched to the Rhine.

BOULOIRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Saint-Calais. The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 10,843 in 1831.—The com., 15 m. E of Le Mans, has a pop. of 2,081.

BOULONNAIS, a district of France, in the ancient prov. of Picardy, now comprised in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais. Area 136,056 hect. The principal towns in this district were Calais, Etaples, Ambleteuse, Guines, Ardres, and the cap., Boulogne.
BOULOU (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. and 5 m. NE of Ceret, on the Tech. Pop. 1,079.
BOULSANE, a river of France, an affluent of the Agly, which it joins on the l. bank, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, after a course of 24 m.
BOULSTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. SE of Haverford-West. Pop. 319.
BOULSTONE, a parish in Herefordshire, in the hund. of Wormelow. Area 750 acres. Pop. 83.
BOULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Rioz. Pop. 743.
BOULTHAM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 2 m. SSW of Lincoln. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 72.
BOULTON, a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter, in Derbyshire, 3 m. SE of Derby. Acres 1,240. Pop. 171.
BOULT-SUR-SUIPPE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Bourgogne. Pop. 1,364.
BOUND BROOK, a village in Somerset co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 33 m. N by E of Trenton.
BOUNDEE. See Bundi.
BOUNOU, a large village in the country of El Dra'ah, in Central Africa, between Zawat and Mimcina.—Caillie.

BOUNTIFUL ISLANDS, two islands forming the E part of the Wellesley group, in the gulf of Carpentaria, on the N coast of Australia. The largest is <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. long, and <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> m. wide; and they are distant 11 m. from each other. They were so named by Flinders, from the supply of turtle he found on them.
BOUNTY ISLES, a cluster of small islets off the S extremity of New Zealand, in 178° 26' E, according to Captain Biscoe.
BOUPE'RE (LE), a commune of France, in Vendee, cant. of Pouzauges. Pop. 2,333.
BOUQUEMAISON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Doullens. Pop. 1,126.
BOURBINCE, a river of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, an affluent of the Arroux, into which it falls, on the l. bank, after a course of 38 m.

BOURBON, a county in the state of Kentucky, U. S., toward the NE. Area 225 sq. m. It is watered by the S fork of Licking river. Pop. 14,478. The cap. is Paris, 36 m. E of Frankfort.—Also a township in Calloway co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 1,100.

BOURBON (ISLE OF), or MASCARENHAS, an island in the Indian ocean, in S lat. 21°, and E long. 55°; about 90 m. WSW of Mauritius; and 370 m. E of Madagascar. When first discovered by the Portuguese, it received the name of Mascarenhas; but this was afterwards changed by the French into Bourbon, and, during the Revolution, into that of the
